author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
body: `FIELD_ID=$(curl ... | jq ...)` runs inside an assignment, so `set -e` won’t reliably abort on curl/jq failure. That can incorrectly fall through with an empty `FIELD_ID` and return exit 3 ("field not found") for network/auth errors, masking real REST failures.

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
body: `HTTP_CODE=$(put_field ...)` is also an assignment, so a curl transport failure may not be caught by `set -e` and can yield an empty/partial `HTTP_CODE`. Handling the command failure explicitly makes REST errors more deterministic and keeps exit-code semantics consistent.

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
body: The comment says “field set and verified”, but the script only checks for HTTP 204 and doesn’t perform a read-back verification. This is slightly misleading for callers relying on the documented guarantees.

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
body: The `cmd1 || cmd2` example will run the second stamp on *any* non-zero exit (including exit 2 “credentials absent” or exit 1 REST errors) and will still return non-zero if the second attempt fails, which conflicts with the “never fail the run over points” guidance. It should only try the fallback name when the first attempt exits 3, and it should swallow non-zero exits in this best-effort step.

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
body: Same issue as step 10a: `cmd1 || cmd2` treats all failures the same and can propagate a non-zero status even though this step is documented as best-effort. Only fall back to the second field name on exit code 3, and otherwise swallow the error to avoid aborting a refine flow that’s supposed to continue without points.
